summary: Gruber reports on Hot Tub, the first hardcore porn app distributed through AltStore in the EU under the Digital Markets Act, and dissects the deliberately misleading framing of it as 'Apple-approved.' He traces Apple's longstanding opposition to pornographic native apps back to a Steve Jobs email and through Phil Schiller's warnings about the DMA's consequences. Apple issued a statement emphasizing it does not approve of the app and is only required by the European Commission to allow alternative marketplaces. Gruber notes the schadenfreude of Apple's notarization process being weaponized to imply editorial approval, while also pointing out that EU officials have been conspicuously silent about this outcome of their own regulation.
key_points: AltStore is misleadingly framing Hot Tub as 'Apple-approved' when Apple merely notarized it — a technical process, not an editorial endorsement
Apple's opposition to native porn apps dates back to Steve Jobs himself, who told a customer 'we do believe we have a moral responsibility to keep porn off the iPhone'
Apple's notarization process has been used inconsistently — rejecting emulators for trademark reasons while being forced to accept porn apps — creating an ironic vulnerability
The EU's DMA compels Apple to allow alternative marketplaces but EU officials have been silent about the pornography consequence, declining to celebrate this 'achievement'
Gruber draws a distinction between Apple 'accepting' apps for notarization versus 'approving' them — begrudging acceptance being fundamentally different from approval
money_quotes: Figuratively speaking, they're not fucking around. Literally speaking, that's exactly what they're aggregating — including an in-app 'teens' channel.
However, we do believe we have a moral responsibility to keep porn off the iPhone. Folks who want porn can buy an Android phone.
Describing Hot Tub as 'Apple-approved' is cheeky, if we're being generous, and downright misleading if we're not.
If we want to get nitty-gritty over verbs, I'd argue that Apple accepts apps — like Hot Tub — for notarization, not approves. Begrudging acceptance is more of a thing than begrudging approval.
There's some undeniable schadenfreude seeing Apple on the wrong end of notarization being used as a tool to convey subject-matter approval.
When it comes to native iOS porn apps, the EU now has an unequivocal advantage over not just the United States but the rest of the world. You'd think they'd want to celebrate the achievement.
I've seen no celebratory tweets or announcements from European Commission officials celebrating the availability of Hot Tub. Just crickets chirping.
key_insight: The EU's DMA has produced exactly the consequence Apple warned about — native porn apps on iOS — while everyone involved plays a cynical game: AltStore exploits notarization to claim Apple approval, and EU officials who mandated this outcome stay conspicuously silent about it.
